pi_items.source: CAMEO Chemicals datasheet states sodium hydroxide 'attacks aluminum and zinc with the evolution of flammable hydrogen gas'; confirmed by multiple chemical compatibility charts rating NaOH as 'D' (severe effect) with aluminum
CAMEO Chemicals indicates caustic solutions corrode metals; industry compatibility standards confirm sodium hydroxide corrodes copper
Industry consensus: anhydrous liquid ammonia is noncorrosive to aluminum alloys; ammonia is only corrosive to copper/brass when moisture is present

Multiple sources confirm: Copper and brass should not be in contact with ammonia as it causes rapid corrosion and stress-corrosion cracking; ammonia reacts with copper alloys especially in presence of moisture
CAMEO Chemicals and chemical safety sources: methyl chloride attacks aluminum in presence of aluminum chloride catalyst, producing pyrophoric trimethylaluminum (spontaneously flammable)
Web search results explicitly state: 'Copper alloys are not compatible with methyl chloride'; Cole-Parmer and engineering sources confirm incompatibility
Chemical compatibility standards and Nickel Institute guidance: aluminum readily attacked in dilute and concentrated sulfuric acid solutions
Industry standards: sulfuric acid corrodes copper; research shows fuming sulfuric acid can trigger vigorous exothermic reactions with copper
Multiple chemical compatibility charts rate toluene as 'A' (excellent) for aluminum compatibility
Chemical compatibility standards rate toluene as 'A' (excellent) for copper and brass compatibility; toluene is a solvent with generally safe metal compatibility

privileged_info_summary: The task tests chemical compatibility knowledge using CAMEO Chemicals data. Verified facts: Sodium hydroxide attacks both aluminum and copper, producing hydrogen gas with aluminum. Anhydrous ammonia is safe for aluminum but attacks copper/brass. Methyl chloride is unsafe for aluminum (forms pyrophoric trimethylaluminum). Sulfuric acid corrodes both aluminum and copper. Toluene is safe for both metals. CRITICAL ERROR DETECTED: The ground truth metadata claims methyl chloride is safe (YES) for copper, but authoritative sources confirm copper alloys are NOT compatible with methyl chloride.
